description: This optical micrograph presents the microstructure of the Fe-7.5Al-0.65C alloy after standard heat treatment at 1100°C for 4 h, which served as the initial condition before the isothermal oxidation experiments. The image reveals a two-phase metallic structure comprising a continuous light-toned ferrite matrix with darker, irregularly shaped Fe3AlCx (kappa-phase) carbide particles distributed throughout the field of view. The carbide phase exhibits varied morphologies, including elongated, plate-like and roughly equiaxed forms, with particle sizes on the order of several micrometres to tens of micrometres as referenced by the 40 μm scale bar. No external oxide scale, internal oxidation, or decarburization is visible because the specimen has only undergone homogenization and has not yet been exposed to the 600–900°C oxidation environment. This baseline microstructural characterization is critical for the paper’s central argument, as it confirms the starting distribution of aluminium-rich carbides within the ferritic matrix, which directly affects aluminium availability for protective Al2O3 or spinel scale formation during subsequent high-temperature oxidation.

This figure displays thermogravimetric oxidation curves of Fe-7.5Al-0.65C alloy isothermally oxidized at 600°C, 700°C, 800°C and 900°C in dry air, with weight gain (mg cm⁻²) plotted against time (h). The curve at 700°C reaches the highest final weight gain of approximately 1.5 mg cm⁻², rising sharply within the first 5 h before plateauing. The 600°C curve shows a persistent, nearly linear increase to about 1.3 mg cm⁻² over 24 h, consistent with the reported breakdown of a simple parabolic rate law and the occurrence of internal oxidation beneath surface nodules. Conversely, the curves at 800°C and 900°C exhibit substantially lower weight gains of roughly 0.7 and 0.4 mg cm⁻² respectively, with the 900°C sample stabilizing most rapidly. This trend demonstrates an inverse temperature dependence above 700°C, where accelerated aluminium diffusion at higher temperatures favors formation of a continuous protective alumina or spinel scale, thereby suppressing nodule growth and reducing the final oxidation rate, whereas at 600°C carbide stability and internal oxidation dominate.

This optical micrograph presents a metallographic cross-section of surface nodules formed on Fe-7.5Al-0.65C alloy after 24 h oxidation at 600°C in dry air. The image reveals hemispherical or irregular nodules protruding from the alloy surface into the overlying oxide scale. Beneath these nodules, a zone of internal oxidation is visible within the ferritic matrix, appearing as dark precipitate-like features extending into the substrate beneath the nodule-alloy interface. The micrograph exhibits distinct contrast variations within the nodules suggestive of a multilayer oxide architecture, while the underlying base metal shows a two-phase microstructure consistent with a ferrite matrix containing dispersed carbide particles. A 50 μm scale bar is included. This figure provides direct morphological evidence that internal oxidation occurs at 600°C localized beneath surface nodules, contrasting with the absence of internal oxidation reported for specimens oxidized at 700, 800 and 900°C. The observed nodule and internal-oxide morphology complements separate EPMA and XRD characterizations indicating outer iron-rich and intermediate aluminium-containing oxide layers, underscoring the temperature-dependent breakdown of protective oxidation behavior in this alloy.

Scanning electron micrograph showing a cross-sectional view of the oxide scale formed on Fe-7.5Al-0.65C alloy after 96 h oxidation at 600°C in dry air. The micrograph reveals a multilayered oxide structure above the alloy substrate. A rough, porous outer oxide layer is visible at the top, marked by label (i). Beneath it lies an intermediate oxide layer containing pores and microcracks, followed by a relatively dense inner oxide layer. Most notably, internal oxidation products are dispersed in the alloy matrix immediately beneath the main oxide scale, manifesting as discrete dark features penetrating into the substrate along an irregular oxide–matrix interface. The scale bar indicates 10 μm. This micrograph directly supports the finding that internal oxidation occurs beneath surface nodules at 600°C, in contrast to specimens oxidized at 700–900°C where no internal oxidation was observed. The morphology demonstrates that at 600°C the aluminium flux is insufficient to form a continuous protective alumina or spinel layer, allowing oxygen to ingress along pores and microcracks and react with aluminium in the substrate.

Figure 5 presents the alloy element concentration profile determined by electron microprobe line scanning across the oxide scale on Fe-7.5Al-0.65C alloy after 96 h oxidation at 600 °C (along line (i) in Fig. 4). The upper curve shows iron content rising sharply from the surface to a peak of 67.2 wt%, then dropping to 43 wt%, rebounding to 53.9 wt% in the inner layer, and finally plateauing at 91.8 wt% in the alloy matrix. The lower curve shows aluminium remaining near zero in the outer region, peaking at 14.5 wt% in the inner layer, and stabilizing at 7.5 wt% in the bulk alloy. A dashed vertical line denotes the oxide–alloy interface. The complementary Fe and Al trends reveal a chemically stratified scale. The accompanying text and related analyses identify the outer, middle and inner layers as Fe2O3, (FexAl1-x)2O3 and an internal Al-rich oxide respectively, confirming that internal oxidation occurs beneath surface nodules at 600 °C while the matrix retains its nominal composition.

This optical micrograph presents a metallographic cross-section of Fe-7.5Al-0.65C alloy after 24 h isothermal oxidation at 700°C in dry air. The image displays prominent surface nodules with an overlying oxide scale and an underlying metallic substrate; the scale bar indicates 50 μm. Visually, the nodules appear as irregular protrusions with a porous outer region and a distinct interface adjacent to the alloy matrix. The caption states that no internal oxidation or decarburization is observed under these conditions, in contrast to the behavior at 600°C. According to the accompanying electron probe microanalysis cited in the text, the nodules comprise an outer porous layer of uniform iron content and an inner layer enriched with aluminium. This micrograph supports the core conclusion that raising the oxidation temperature to 700°C suppresses internal oxidation through the formation of a protective healing layer of Al₂O₃ or FeAl₂O₄, which acts as a barrier to outward iron cation diffusion and marks a transition from internal to predominantly external oxidation in this alloy system.

This optical micrograph shows a metallographic cross-section of the oxide scale formed on Fe-7.5Al-0.65C alloy after isothermal oxidation at 900°C for 24 h in dry air. The image displays the interface between the upper oxide region and the underlying metallic substrate, which exhibits a two-phase microstructure consistent with a ferrite matrix containing carbide particles. A scale bar indicates 200 μm. In agreement with the caption and cited text, no surface nodules or internal oxidation products are visible at or beneath the oxide–matrix interface. This contrasts sharply with specimens oxidized at 600, 700, and 800°C, where nodules and/or internal oxidation were observed. The micrograph provides direct microstructural evidence that, at 900°C, enhanced aluminium mobility enables the formation of a continuous, protective Al₂O₃ or FeAl₂O₄ spinel layer that suppresses outward iron cation diffusion, inhibits nodule growth, and prevents internal oxidation, thereby lowering the overall oxidation rate relative to the lower-temperature conditions.
